prompt:
A TablePay Restaurant Ordering & Community system with main features including offline payment capture and reconciliation, secure refund handling, rich dish and option modeling, high-concurrency ordering performance, and community engagement with governance. Customers use an English UI rendered server-side and progressively enhanced with HTMX so menu browsing, option selection, and cart updates feel instant without full page reloads; each dish can be filtered by category, tags, availability windows (for example, “Lunch 11:00 AM–2:30 PM”), and sold-out status, with clear required-option prompts for spice level, soup base, or portion size before checkout. Store Managers publish or unpublish items, set sort order, manage images, and configure pricing strategies such as add-ons and size upcharges; image uploads validate file type and size (JPEG/PNG only, max 2 MB) and show immediate preview and error feedback. Finance Admins work from a payments workspace that supports “gateway-like” flows in a fully offline environment: the system can run against a locally hosted WeChat Pay (JSAPI)-compatible simulator for testing and training, and it can also ingest real-world payment statements exported from terminals as CSV for reconciliation, showing variances, exception logs, and an operator workflow to resolve mismatches. Community features allow logged-in users to like, favorite, and comment on dishes or posts, report abusive content, and block other users; Moderators review a queue of flagged items with reason codes and outcomes, while the UI throttles rapid actions and clearly communicates when a user hits a posting cooldown.

The backend uses Flask to expose REST-style endpoints consumed by HTMX requests, enforcing server-side validation on every parameter and returning consistent, human-readable error states; SQLite persists users, roles, dishes, options, inventory state, payments, refunds, reconciliation runs, and moderation events entirely on local disk. Authentication is local username and password only, with bcrypt password hashing, a minimum of 12 characters, lockout after 10 failed attempts in 15 minutes, and CSRF protection on cookie-based sessions; anti-replay uses per-request nonces with a 5-minute expiration for sensitive operations such as refunds and role changes. Payment callbacks are supported without internet dependency by accepting signed “callback packages” generated by the local gateway simulator or imported from files; signature verification uses pre-shared rotating keys stored encrypted at rest, and idempotency prevents duplicate callbacks using a 24-hour uniqueness window per transaction reference. Refunds support partial and multiple refunds up to the captured amount, enforce original-route refund rules, and trigger step-up confirmation requiring re-entry of the manager password when the refund exceeds $50.00 or when risk checks detect anomalies such as more than 3 refunds from the same device in 30 minutes. High availability is achieved through async job processing backed by a SQLite job table for tasks like reconciliation and bulk menu updates, plus caching with short TTLs (for example, 60 seconds for menu reads), rate limiting (for example, 60 requests per minute per user), circuit breaking for overloaded endpoints, structured exception logging, nightly encrypted backups retained for 14 days, and a tested restore procedure that can rebuild the system on a new machine with no network access.
